instruction:
This question paper contains three sections - A, B and C. Each part is compulsory.
 Section - A has 20 MCQs, attempt any 16 out of 20.
 Section - B has 20 MCQs, attempt any 16 out of 20
 Section - C has 10 MCQs, attempt any 8 out of 10.
 There is no negative marking.
 All questions carry equal marks.

questions.question: The number of proper subsets of the set {1, 2, 3} is :
questions.question_raw: The number of proper subsets of the set {1, 2, 3} is :
questions.explanation:
The no. of proper subsets = 2^n - 1 = 2^3 - 1 = 7
Here n = no of elements of given set = 3.

questions.question: Projection (the foot of perpendicular) from (x, y) on the x-axis is
questions.question_raw: Projection (the foot of perpendicular) from (x, y) on the x-axis is
questions.explanation:
Let L be the foot of the perpendicular from the X-axis. Therefore its y coordinate is zero.
Therefore the coordinates of the point L is (x, 0).

questions.question: (0.15 + 0.015 + 0.0015 + ... to 8 terms) = ?
questions.explanation:
Here, we have a = 0.15 and $r=\frac{0.015}{0.15}=\frac{0.015}{0.015 \times 10}=\frac{1}{10}<1$
$\therefore S_{n}=\frac{a\left(1-r^{n}\right)}{(1-r)}=\frac{0.15\left[1-\frac{1}{10^{n}}\right]}{\left(1-\frac{1}{10}\right)}=\left(\frac{15}{100} \times \frac{10}{9}\right)\left(1-\frac{1}{10^{n}}\right)=\frac{1}{6}\left(1-\frac{1}{10^{n}}\right)$
$\therefore S_{8}=\frac{1}{6}\left(1-\frac{1}{10^{8}}\right)$

questions.question: A line is equally inclined to the axis and the length of perpendicular from the origin upon the line is $\sqrt 2 $. A possible equation of the line is
questions.question_raw: A line is equally inclined to the axis and the length of perpendicular from the origin upon the line is {tex}\sqrt 2 {/tex}. A possible equation of the line is
questions.explanation:
Since the line is equally inclined, the slope of the line should be -1, because it makes 135^o in the positive direction of the X-axis.
This implies the equation of the line is y = -x + c 
i.e; x + y - c = 0
distance of the line from the origin is given as $\sqrt 2$ 
Therefore $\sqrt 2$ = $|c|\over \sqrt{1^2+1^2}$ 
This implies c = 2
Hence, the equation of the line is x + y = 2

questions.question: If $\alpha$ is a repeated root of ax^2 + bx + c = 0, then $\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow \alpha} \frac{\tan \left(a x^{2}+b x+c\right)}{(x-\alpha)^{2}}$ is
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\alpha{/tex} is a repeated root of ax2 + bx + c = 0, then {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow \alpha} \frac{\tan \left(a x^{2}+b x+c\right)}{(x-\alpha)^{2}}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
$\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow \alpha}\left[\frac{\tan \left(a x^{2}+b x+c\right)}{(x-\alpha)^{2}}\right]$
$=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow \alpha}\left[\frac{\tan \{a(x-\alpha)(x-\alpha)\}}{a(x-\alpha)^{2}}\right] \times a$
$=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow \alpha}\left[\frac{\tan \left\{a(x-\alpha)^{2}\right\}}{a(x-\alpha)^{2}}\right] \times a$
$=1 \times a\left[\lim \limits_{\theta \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{\tan \theta}{\theta}\right)=1\right]$
= a

questions.explanation: Most repeated value is the mode. Here it is 5

B = {x : x is odd and x $> $1}

correct_option_texts: B = {x : x is odd and x $ \in $ Z}

If we add an odd number and 1 then we get even number
$\therefore$ B = {x : x is odd and x $ \in $ Z}

questions.question: If p be the length of the perpendicular from the origin on the line $\frac {x} {a} + \frac {y} {b} = 1$, then
questions.question_raw: If p be the length of the perpendicular from the origin on the line {tex}\frac {x} {a} + \frac {y} {b} = 1{/tex}, then
questions.explanation:
It is given that p is the length of the perpendicular from the origin on the line
$\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=1$
$\frac{1}{a} x+\frac{1}{b}$ y - 1 = 0
Using the formula of the distance we get, 
$\therefore p=\left|\frac{0+0-1}{\sqrt{\frac{1}{a^{2}}+\frac{1}{b^{2}}}}\right|$
Squaring both sides,
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{p^{2}}=\frac{1}{a^{2}}+\frac{1}{b^{2}}$

questions.question: The area of triangle formed by the lines y = x, y = 2x and y = 3x + 4 is
questions.question_raw: The area of triangle formed by the lines y = x, y = 2x and y = 3x + 4 is
questions.explanation:
Area of the triangle formed by the coordinates (x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2) and (x_3, y_3) is given by
$1\over 2$[x_1(y_2 - y_3) + x_2(y_3 - y_1) + x_3(y_1 - y_2)]
On solving the lines 1 and 2, the point of intersection is (0, 0)
On solving the lines 2 and 3, the point of intersection is (4, 8)
On solving the line 3 and 1 , the point of intersection is (-2, -2)
Now substituting the values to find the area of the triangle,
Area = $1\over 2$| [0 + 4(-2 - 0) + (-2)(0 - 8)] |
= 4 sq units

questions.question: $\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left\{\frac{1}{h^{} \sqrt[3]{8+h}}-\frac{1}{2 h}\right\}=$
questions.question_raw: {tex}\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left\{\frac{1}{h^{} \sqrt[3]{8+h}}-\frac{1}{2 h}\right\}={/tex}
questions.explanation:
$\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{1}{h \sqrt[3]{8+h}}-\frac{1}{2 h}\right]$
$=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{1}{h}\left\{\frac{1}{\sqrt[3]{8+h}}-\frac{1}{2}\right\}\right]$
$=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{1}{h}\left\{\frac{2-(8+h)^{1 / 3}}{2 \times \sqrt[3]{8+h}}\right\}\right]$
$=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{1}{h}\left\{\frac{8^{1 / 3}-(8+h)^{1 / 3}}{2 \sqrt[3]{8+h}}\right\}\right]$ [A^3 - B^3 = (A - B)(A^2 + AB + B^2) or A - B $\frac{A^{3}-B^{3}}{A^{2}+A B+B^{2}}$]
$=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{8-(8+h)}{h\{2 \sqrt[3]{8+h}\}\left\{4+2(8+h)^{1 / 3}+(8+h)^{2 / 3}\right\}}\right]$
$=\left[\frac{-1}{2 \times \sqrt[3]{8}\left\{4+2 \times 8^{1 / 3}+8^{2 / 3}\right\}}\right]$
$=\frac{-1}{2 \times 2(4+4+4)}$
$=\frac{-1}{48}$

questions.question: If the mean of the squares of first n natural numbers be 11, then n is equal to
questions.question_raw: If the mean of the squares of first n natural numbers be 11, then n is equal to
questions.explanation:
Mean = $\frac{{\frac{{n(n + 1)(2n + 1)}}{6}}}{n} = \frac{{(n + 1)(2n + 1)}}{6}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 11 = $\frac{(n+1)(2 n+1)}{6}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 66 = (n + 1)(2n + 1)
$\Rightarrow$ 2n^2 + 3n - 65 = 0
$\Rightarrow$ 2n^2 + 13n - 10n - 65 = 0
$\Rightarrow$ (2n + 13)(n - 5) = 0
$\Rightarrow$ n = 5, $\frac{-13}{2}$ 
So, n = 5

questions.question: The foot of the perpendicular from (2, 3) on the line 3x + 4y – 6 = 0 is
questions.question_raw: The foot of the perpendicular from (2, 3) on the line 3x + 4y – 6 = 0 is
questions.explanation:
The equation of the line perpendicular to the given line 3x + 4y = 6 is 4x - 3y + k = 0
Since this line passes through (2, 3)
4(2) - 3(3) + k = 0
Therefore k = 1
Therefore the line which is perpendicular to the given line is 4x - 3y + 1 = 0
On solving both the equations we get, x = $\frac{14}{25}$ and y = $\frac{27}{25}$
Hence the foot of the perpendicular is ($\frac{14}{25}$, $\frac{27}{25}$)

questions.question: The sum of the infinite geometric series $\left(1-\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{3^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{3}}+\ldots \infty\right)=$ ?
questions.question_raw: The sum of the infinite geometric series {tex}\left(1-\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{3^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{3}}+\ldots \infty\right)={/tex} ?
questions.explanation:
Given, a = 1 and R = $\frac{-1}{3}$. Clearly, |r| = $\frac{1}{3}<1$.
$\therefore S_{\infty}=\frac{a}{(1-r)}=\frac{1}{\left(1+\frac{1}{3}\right)}=\frac{3}{4}$.

questions.question: The line which passes through the point ( 0 , 1 ) and perpendicular to the line x – 2y + 11 = 0 is
questions.question_raw: The line which passes through the point ( 0 , 1 ) and perpendicular to the line x – 2y + 11 = 0 is
questions.explanation:
The line which is perpendicular to the given line is 2x + y + k = 0
Since it passes through (0,1)
2(0) + 1 + k = 0
This implies k = -1
Hence the equation of the required line is 2x + y - 1 = 0

R.H.L = $\underset{x \rightarrow 0}{lim}[\cos (x+h)]$ = 0

questions.explanation:

So, 
3(mode + 2mean) $\mu$ = mode + 2mean
$\mu$ = $\frac{1}{3}$

questions.question: If f(x) = $\frac{1}{\sqrt{x-|x|}}$, then D_f is equal to
questions.question_raw: If f(x) = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{x-|x|}}{/tex}, then Df is equal to
questions.explanation:
This function is defined only if 
x - |x| > 0
$\Rightarrow$ x > |x|
Which is not possible because
|x| $\ge$ x
so, $x \in \phi $

questions.question: The straight lines x + y - 4 = 0, 3x + y – 4 = 0, x - 3y – 4 = 0 form a triangle which is
questions.question_raw: The straight lines x + y - 4 = 0, 3x + y – 4 = 0, x - 3y – 4 = 0 form a triangle which is
questions.explanation:
The triangle formed by these lines is a right-angled triangle.
If the lines are perpendicular to each other, then the product of their slopes is -1
The slope of lines 3x + y – 4 = 0, x - 3y – 4 = 0 are -3 and $\frac{1}{3}$ respectively.
The product of the slopes is -1.
Hence these two lines are perpendicular to each other.
This infers that the triangle formed by these lines is a right angled triangle.

questions.question: $1+\sqrt{3}+3+3 \sqrt{3}$ + ... upto 10 terms = ?
questions.question_raw: {tex}1+\sqrt{3}+3+3 \sqrt{3}{/tex} + ... upto 10 terms = ?
questions.explanation:
Here, a = 1 and r = $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{1}$ = $\sqrt{3}$ > 1.
$\therefore S_{n}=\frac{a\left(r^{n}-1\right)}{(r-1)} \Rightarrow S_{10}=\frac{a\left(r^{10}-1\right)}{(r-1)}=\frac{1 \times\left[(\sqrt{3})^{10}-1\right]}{(\sqrt{3}-1)}=\frac{\left(3^{5}-1\right)}{(\sqrt{3}-1)} \times \frac{(\sqrt{3}+1)}{(\sqrt{3}+1)}$
$\Rightarrow S_{10}=\frac{(243-1)}{2} \times(\sqrt{3}+1)=\frac{242}{2} \cdot(\sqrt{3}+1)$ = $121(\sqrt{3}+1)$.

questions.question: $\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\sec ^{2} x-2}{\tan x-1}$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\sec ^{2} x-2}{\tan x-1}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
Given, $\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\sec ^{2} x-2}{\tan x-1}=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{1+\tan ^{2} x-2}{\tan x-1}$
$=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\tan ^{2} x-1}{\tan x-1}=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{(\tan x+1)(\tan x-1)}{(\tan x-1)}$
$=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}}(\tan x+1)=\tan \frac{\pi}{4}+1=1+1=2$

questions.question: Mean deviation for n observations x_1, x_2, ..., x_n from their mean $\bar x$ is given by
questions.question_raw: Mean deviation for n observations x1, x2, ..., xn from their mean {tex}\bar x{/tex} is given by
questions.explanation:
We know for n observations x_1, x_2, ..., x_n having $\bar x$ is given by
M.D = $\frac{\sum {d}_{{i}}}{{n}}$
But we know
d_i = $\left|\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}-\overline{\mathrm{x}}\right|$
So mean deviation becomes,
M.D = $\frac{\sum\left|{x}_{{i}}-\overline{{x}}\right|}{{n}}$
Or
M.D = $\frac{1}{{n}} \sum_\limits{{i}=0}^{{n}}\left|{x}_{{i}}-\overline{{x}}\right|$

questions.question: The distance between the lines 5x – 12y + 65 = 0 and 5x – 12y – 39 = 0
questions.question_raw: The distance between the lines 5x – 12y + 65 = 0 and 5x – 12y – 39 = 0
questions.explanation:
To find the distance between two parallel lines given by the equations $A x+B y+C_1=0$ and $A x+B y+C_2=0$, we can use the formula:
$ \text { Distance }=\frac{\left|C_2-C_1\right|}{\sqrt{A^2+B^2}} $
For the lines $5 x-12 y+65=0$ and $5 x-12 y-39=0$ : - Here, $A=5, B=-12, C_1=65$, and $C_2=-39$. Now, we calculate the distance:
$ \text { Distance }=\frac{|-39-65|}{\sqrt{5^2+(-12)^2}}=\frac{|-104|}{\sqrt{25+144}}=\frac{104}{\sqrt{169}}=\frac{104}{13}=8 $

questions.question: If 1 + $\frac{1+2}{2}$ + $\frac{1+2+3}{3}$ + ...... to n terms is S. Then, S is equal to
questions.question_raw: If 1 + {tex}\frac{1+2}{2}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1+2+3}{3}{/tex} + ...... to n terms is S. Then, S is equal to
questions.explanation:
Let T_n be the nth term of the given series.
Thus, we have:
T_n = $\frac{1+2+3+4+5+\ldots+n}{n}$ = $\frac{n(n+1)}{2 n}$ = $\frac{n}{2}+\frac{1}{2}$
Now, sum of n terms is,
S_n be the sum of n terms of the given series.
Thus, we have:
S_n = $\sum_{k=1}^{n}$ $\left(\frac{k}{2}+\frac{1}{2}\right)$
$\Rightarrow$ S_n = $\sum_{k=1}^{n}$ $\frac{k}{2}+\frac{n}{2}$
$\Rightarrow$ S_n = $\frac{n(n+1)}{4}$ + $\frac{n}{2}$
$\Rightarrow$ S_n = $\frac{n}{2}$ $\left(\frac{n+1}{2}+1\right)$
$\Rightarrow$ S_n = $\frac{n}{2}$ $\left(\frac{n+3}{2}\right)$
$\Rightarrow$ S_n = $\frac{n(n+3)}{4}$

questions.question: The mean deviation of the numbers 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 from the mean is
questions.question_raw: The mean deviation of the numbers 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 from the mean is
questions.explanation:
Mean (X) = $\frac{3+4+5+6+7}{5}$
= $\frac{25}{5}$ = 5
Taking the absolute value of deviation of each term from the mean, we get:
MD = $\frac{|(3-5)|+|(4-5)|+|(5-5)|+|(6-5)|+|(7-5)|}{5}$
= $\frac{2+1+0+1+2}{5}$
= $\frac{6}{5}$
= 1.2

questions.question: If ${\left( { - 1 + \sqrt 3 i} \right)^n} + {\left( { - 1 - \sqrt 3 i} \right)^n} = {2^n}\alpha $ then $\alpha $ is equal to :
questions.question_raw: If {tex}{\left( { - 1 + \sqrt 3 i} \right)^n} + {\left( { - 1 - \sqrt 3 i} \right)^n} = {2^n}\alpha {/tex} then {tex}\alpha {/tex} is equal to :
questions.explanation:
We have $-1+i \sqrt{3}=2 \omega$ and $-1-i \sqrt{3}=2 \omega^{2}$ 
Now 
$(-1+\sqrt{3} i)^{n}+(-1-\sqrt{3} i)^{n}$ = $(2 \omega)^{n}+\left(2 \omega^{2}\right)^{n}$ = $2^{n}\left[(\omega)^{n}+\left(\omega^{2}\right)^{n}\right]$

questions.question: The minimum value of the expression 3^x + 3^1 - x, x $ \in $ R, is
questions.question_raw: The minimum value of the expression 3x + 31 - x, x {tex} \in {/tex} R, is
questions.explanation:
We know A.M. $\ge$ G.M. for positive numbers.
Thus, $\frac{3^{x}+3^{1-x}}{2} \geq \sqrt{3^{x} \cdot 3^{1-x}}$
$\Rightarrow$ $\frac{3^{x}+3^{1-x}}{2} \geq \sqrt{3^{x} \cdot \frac{3}{3^{x}}}$
$\Rightarrow$ $3^{x}+3^{1-x} \geq 2 \sqrt{3}$.

Here, we see that A - B = A$\cap$B
$\therefore$ this option is false.

questions.question: A relation R is defined from {2, 3, 4, 5} to {3, 6, 7, 10} by: x R y $\Leftrightarrow$ x is relatively prime to y. Then domain of R is
questions.question_raw: A relation R is defined from {2, 3, 4, 5} to {3, 6, 7, 10} by: x R y {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x is relatively prime to y. Then domain of R is
questions.explanation:
Relatively prime numbers are those numbers that have only 1 as the common factor.
So, according to this definition we get to know that (2, 3), (2, 7), (3, 7), (3, 10), (4, 3), (4, 7), (5, 3), (5, 6), (5, 7) are relatively prime.
So, R = {(2, 3), (2, 7), (3, 7), (3, 10), (4, 3), (4, 7), (5, 3), (5, 6) ,(5, 7)}.
Therefore, the Domain of R is the values of x or the first element of the ordered pair.
So, Domain = {2, 3, 4, 5}

questions.question: Mark the correct answer for: i^91 = ?
questions.question_raw: Mark the correct answer for: i91 = ?
questions.explanation: i^91 = (i^4)^22 $\times$i^3 = 1$\times$i^2 = 1$\times$(-1)$\times$i = -i

questions.question: The number of numbers between x and x^2 which are divisible by x is
questions.question_raw: The number of numbers between x and x2 which are divisible by x is
questions.explanation:
The required sequence is 2x, 3x, 4x ... (x - 1)x
Hence we get T_1 = a = 2x and T_n = (x - 1)x
So using the nth term formula for A.P
T_n = a + (n - 1)d 
$\Rightarrow$ x(x - 1) = 2x + (n - 1)x
$\Rightarrow$ x - 1 = (n + 1)
$\Rightarrow$ n = x - 2
Hence the number of numbers between x and x^2 which are divisible by x is x - 2.

questions.question: If f : N $ \times $ N $ \to $N is such that f(m, n) = m + n where N is the set of natural number, then which of the following is true?
questions.question_raw: If f : N {tex} \times {/tex} N {tex} \to {/tex}N is such that f(m, n) = m + n where N is the set of natural number, then which of the following is true?

options.text: f is one-one but not onto
f is onto but not one-one
f is neither one-one nor onto

correct_option_texts: f is neither one-one nor onto
questions.explanation:
One - One: f(1, 2) = 1 + 2 = 3; f(2, 1) = 2 + 1
$\therefore$ f is not one - one
onto: f(1, 1) = 2, f(1, 2) = 3, f(2, 1) = 3
f(1, 3) = 4 = f(3, 1) and so on
$\therefore$ range of f = {2, 3, 4, 5, ....} $\ne$ N
$\therefore$ f is not onto.

questions.question: If z $=\frac{1}{1-\cos \theta-i \sin \theta}$, then Re (z) =
questions.question_raw: If z {tex}=\frac{1}{1-\cos \theta-i \sin \theta}{/tex}, then Re (z) =
questions.explanation:
$\frac{1}{2}$
$z=\frac{1}{1-\cos \theta-i \sin \theta}$
$z=\frac{1}{1-\cos \theta-i \sin \theta}$ $\times \frac{1-\cos \theta+i \sin \theta}{1-\cos \theta+i \sin \theta}$
$\Rightarrow z=\frac{1-\cos \theta+i \sin \theta}{(1-\cos \theta)^{2}-(i \sin \theta)^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow z=\frac{1-\cos \theta+i \sin \theta}{1+\cos ^{2} \theta-2 \cos \theta+\sin ^{2} \theta}$
$\Rightarrow z=\frac{1-\cos \theta+i \sin \theta}{1+1-2 \cos \theta}$
$\Rightarrow z=\frac{1-\cos \theta+i \sin \theta}{2(1-\cos \theta)}$
$\Rightarrow$ Re (z) $=\frac{(1-\cos \theta)}{2(1-\cos \theta)}=\frac{1}{2}$

questions.question: Three numbers are in AP whose sum is 24 and whose product is 440. The largest of these numbers is
questions.question_raw: Three numbers are in AP whose sum is 24 and whose product is 440. The largest of these numbers is
questions.explanation:
Let the numbers be (a - d), a and (a + d). Then,
$\Rightarrow$ (a - d) + a + (a + d) = 24 $\Rightarrow$ 3a = 24 $\Rightarrow$ a =8
And,( 8 - d) $\times$ 8 $\times$ (8 + d) = 440 $\Rightarrow$ (64 - d^2) = 55 $\Rightarrow$ $d^{2}=$ 9 $\Rightarrow$ d = $\pm$3.
Therefore, the numbers are {(8 - 3) , 8 and (8 + 3)} or {(8+ 3), 8 and (8 - 3)} i.e 5, 8, 11 and 11, 8, 5.
The required largest of these numbers is 11.

questions.question: Regression coefficient of X on Y is 0.25, then the regression coefficient of Y on X is
questions.question_raw: Regression coefficient of X on Y is 0.25, then the regression coefficient of Y on X is
questions.explanation:
Here,
${b_{xy}} = r.\frac{{{\sigma _x}}}{{{\sigma _y}}} = 0.25$ $ \Rightarrow \frac{{{\sigma _x}}}{{{\sigma _y}}} = \frac{{0.25}}{r}$ 
Now, ${b_{yx}} = r.\frac{{{\sigma _y}}}{{{\sigma _x}}} = r.\frac{r}{{0.25}} = {r^2} \times 4$ 
As, $0 < {r^2} \le 1$ 
So, $[\begin{array}{l} 0 < 4{r^2} \le 4\\ 0 < {b_{yx}} \le 4 \end{array}$

questions.question:
Instructions for Question 46 to 50:
An equation of the form ax^2 + bx + c, a $\neq$ 0 is called quadratic equation in variable x, where a, b and c are numbers (real or complex).
The roots of quadratic equation ax^2 + bx + c = 0, a $\neq$ 0 are
$\alpha=\frac{-b+\sqrt{b^{2}-4 a c}}{2 a}$
and $\beta=\frac{-b-\sqrt{b^{2}-4 a c}}{2 a}$
Now, if we look at these roots of quadratic equation
ax^2 + bx + c = 0; a $\neq$ 0, we observe that the roots depend upon the value of the quantity b^2 - 4ac. This quantity is known as the discriminant of the quadratic equation and denoted by D.
There are following cases:
Case I If b^2 - 4ac = 0 i.e. D = 0, then $\alpha=\beta=-\frac{b}{2 a}$
Thus, if b^2 - 4ac = 0, then the quadratic equation has real and equal roots and each equal to $\frac {-b}{2a}$.
Case II If a, b and c are rational numbers and b^2 - 4ac > 0 and it is a perfect square, then D = $\sqrt{b^{2}-4 a c}$ is a rational number and hence $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are rational and unequal.
Case III If b^2 - 4ac > 0 and it is not a perfect square, then roots are irrational and unequal.
Case IV If b^2 - 4ac < 0, then the roots are complex conjugate of each other.
Roots of quadratic equation 3x^2 - 4x + $\frac{20}{3}$ = 0 are given by
